coredata.dc:title: Design and preparation of a theranostic peptideticle for targeted cancer therapy: Peptide-based codelivery of doxorubicin/curcumin and graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Moasses Ghafary, Soroush
Rahimjazi, Elnaz
Hamzehil, Hadiseh
Modarres Mousavi, Sayed Mostafa
Nikkhah, Maryam
Hosseinkhani, Saman
coredata.dc:description: Although chemotherapy has been known as a powerful medication for cancer treatment over the years, there is an important necessity for designing a novel targeted drug delivery system to overcome the drawbacks of this conventional method including undesired side effects on normal cells and drug resistance. The structural differences between the surface of cancerous and normal cells allow to design and engineer targeted drug delivery systems for cancer treatment. Integrins as one of the cell surface receptors over-expressed in cancer cells could potentially be suitable candidates for targeting cancer cells. In the present study, the novel nano-carriers based on designed MiRGD peptides and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used for targeted delivery of doxorubicin (Dox) and curcumin (Cur) as hydrophilic and hydrophobic drug models, respectively. The prepared nano-composites were characterized by UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopies, Zeta-Sizer and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Altogether, the results of cellular uptake and fluorimetric assays performed in HUVEC and HFF cells as models of αv integrin-over-expressed cancer and normal cells, respectively, besides in-vivo study on breast cancer bearing BALB/c mice, demonstrated that the prepared nano-composites can be considered as suitable multifunctional theranostic peptideticles for targeted drug delivery and tracking.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Theranostic
Drug delivery
Graphene quantum dots
In vivo imaging

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Theranostics, Targeted Drug Delivery, MiRGD Peptide, Graphene QDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Expression and purification of MiRGD peptide
Investigation of the formation of MiRGD-Dox/MiRGD-Cur complexes
Encapsulation efficiency of drugs in MiRGD
In-vivo bio distribution and tumor targeting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The chemical structure of doxorubicin and (B) enol and keto forms of curcumin. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectrum (D) FTIR spectra (E) photoluminescence spectra of GQDs.

The emission spectrum of (A) Cur (54.2 μM) and MiRGD-Cur at excitation wavelength of 430 nm and (B) Dox (36 μM) and MiRGD-Dox at excitation wavelength of 480 nm with different concentrations (10.3, 41.5 and 72.7 μM) of MiRGD peptide, (C) the MiRGD-Cur-GQDs and (D) MiRGD-Dox-GQDs complexes with three different ratios of components (shown in Table 1s), by excitation at 360 nm. (E) ζ-potential of MiRGD peptide, GQDs, and Dox/Cur -MiRGD -GQDs complexes. (F) TEM images of (I) GQDs, (II) MiRGD-Dox-GQDs and (III) MiRGD-Cur-GQDs nano-composites.
The release profile of Dox and Cur from Dox/Cur-MiRGD peptide-GQDs under two different pH values of 7.4 and 5.5 at 37 °C.

Fluorescence microscopy of treated HUVEC cells with (A) Dox-MiRGD-GQDs nanocomposites, (B) Cur-MiRGD-GQDs nanocomposites, (C) Dox and Cur alone and (D) GQDs at two different concentrations and untreated cells as control are shown in different panels. In panel A: left top is bright field, right top is detected Dox in the uptaken nanocomposites in the green spectral region, left bottom is detected GQDs in the uptaken nanocomposites in the blue spectral region and overlay is indicated in right bottom. In panel B: left is bright field and right is detected Cur and GQDs in the uptaken nanocomposites in the blue spectral region. In panel C: left top and bottom are bright field, right top and bottom are detected Dox and Cur alone in the green and blue spectral region, respectively. In panel D: left top and bottom are bright field, right top and bottom are detected GQDs alone at two maximum (50 μl of 200 μg/ml) and minimum (10 μl of 200 μg/ml) amounts used in the preparation of nanocomposites in the blue spectral region, respectively. In panel E: left top and bottom are bright field and right top and bottom are merged images of peptide treated and untreated cells as negative controls captured in the green and blue spectral region.

Fluorescence microscopy of treated HFF cells with (A) Dox-MiRGD-GQDs nanocomposites, (B) Cur-MiRGD-GQDs nanocomposites, (C) Dox and Cur alone and (D) GQDs at two different concentrations and untreated cells as control are shown in different panels. In panel A: left top is bright field, right top is detected Dox in the uptaken nanocomposites in the green spectral region, left bottom is detected GQDs in the uptaken nanocomposites in the blue spectral region and overlay is indicated in right bottom. In panel B: left is bright field and right is detected Cur and GQDs in the uptaken nanocomposites in the blue spectral region. In panel C: left top and bottom are bright field, right top and bottom are detected Dox and Cur alone in the green and blue spectral region, respectively. In panel D: left top and bottom are bright field, right top and bottom are detected GQDs alone at two maximum (50 μl of 200 μg/ml) and minimum (10 μl of 200 μg/ml) amounts used in the preparation of nanocomposites in the blue spectral region, respectively. In panel E: left top and bottom are bright field and right top and bottom are merge images of peptide treated and untreated cells as negative controls captured in the green and blue spectral region.

Quantitative fluorimetric assay of treated (A) HUVEC and (B) HFF cells with Cur-MiRGD-GQDs nano-composites 2 and 3, Dox-MiRGD-GQDs nano-composites 2 and 3, Cur, Dox, GQDs and peptide as well as untreated cells using scanning area mode at three different maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs (ex: 360 nm, em: 390 nm), Dox (ex: 480 nm, em: 600 nm) and Cur (ex: 430 nm, em: 540 nm). The 10-fold intensity of each fluorescence signal is presented for ex: 480 nm, em: 600 nm.

In-vivo fluorescence imaging. (A) The fluorescence imaging of Dox peptideticle in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice within 2.5 h post injection (in the light exposure time of 5 s). (B) Fluorescence imaging of major organs and tumor tissues ex-vivo.

head.ce:title: Design and preparation of a theranostic peptideticle for targeted cancer therapy: Peptide-based codelivery of doxorubicin/curcumin and graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanobiothechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nanobiothechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanobiothechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomaterials, Faculty of Interdisciplinary Sciences and Technology, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Interdisciplinary Sciences and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bimaterials, Faculty of Interdisciplinary Sciences and Technology, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Biological Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: They have equal contribution in this work and the data are collected from their thesis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although chemotherapy has been known as a powerful medication for cancer treatment over the years, there is an important necessity for designing a novel targeted drug delivery system to overcome the drawbacks of this conventional method including undesired side effects on normal cells and drug resistance. The structural differences between the surface of cancerous and normal cells allow to design and engineer targeted drug delivery systems for cancer treatment. Integrins as one of the cell surface receptors over-expressed in cancer cells could potentially be suitable candidates for targeting cancer cells. In the present study, the novel nano-carriers based on designed MiRGD peptides and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used for targeted delivery of doxorubicin (Dox) and curcumin (Cur) as hydrophilic and hydrophobic drug models, respectively. The prepared nano-composites were characterized by UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopies, Zeta-Sizer and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Altogether, the results of cellular uptake and fluorimetric assays performed in HUVEC and HFF cells as models of αv integrin-over-expressed cancer and normal cells, respectively, besides in-vivo study on breast cancer bearing BALB/c mice, demonstrated that the prepared nano-composites can be considered as suitable multifunctional theranostic peptideticles for targeted drug delivery and tracking.

Schematic depiction of assembling Dox/Cur-MiRGD-GQDs peptideticles through noncovalent interactions, transfection into αv integrin-positive HUVEC cells and intravenous injection into 4T1-induced breast cancer BALB/c mice.
Unlabelled Image

ce:sections.ce:para:
Cancer is the second most common cause of mortality in the world after heart diseases, which is developed by uncontrolled growth of abnormal cells in the body. 1 Chemotherapy is a currently effective type of cancer treatment approach that uses anti-cancer drugs to kill cancerous cells. However, a lot of patients under treatment with conventional chemotherapy suffer from hair loss, heart and lung damages, kidney and bladder problems, etc. Targeted drug delivery has attracted a lot of attention because of the drawbacks of chemotherapeutic drugs including undesired side effects on normal cells and subsequent drug resistance. 2 Therefore, today, several creative methods and a wide variety of nanocarriers based on lipids, proteins, peptides and different classes of organic and inorganic nanoparticles have been developed for anti-cancer drug delivery to overcome the problems associated with conventional chemotherapy, as well as limitations of physicochemical properties of some therapeutic agents such as low solubility, stability and biocompatibility. 3–5 Among this, chimeric peptides can be considered as one of the best attractive candidates for drug delivery due to their good biocompatibility and biodegradability, high drug loading capacity, cell-specific targeting and controlled drug delivery into targeted sites. 6
,
7 We have designed different chimeric peptides with various functional units suitable for gene delivery in-vitro conditions. 8–14 Therefore, in this study we have assessed the ability of one of the previous reported chimeric peptides (MiRGD) designed for gene delivery for targeted drug delivery in order to overcome the challenges associated with the effective chemotherapeutic drug delivery. 11

MiRGD peptide is a targeted multifunctional peptide with four different motifs containing a targeting iRGD (CRGDKGPDC) motif, which is a 9-amino acid cyclic peptide located in the N-terminal of MiRGD and provides deep penetration into cancerous tissue by binding to αv integrin, overexpressed receptor in tumor cells. 11 MiRGD peptide containing cyclic peptide (iRGD) and also some hydrophobic amino acids, as well as positively charged residues embedded in the previously mentioned other three motifs 8–16 is considered for possible delivery of both hydrophobic and hydrophilic drugs.

To date, many different types of chemotherapeutic drugs including doxorubicin (Dox), tamoxifen, paclitaxel, and cisplatin have been investigated with different kinds of carriers in order to increase the drug concentration in tumor sites and reduce their adverse side effects. 17–19 To the best of our knowledge, there is no any organized study on using multi-functional chimeric peptides as a carrier for drug delivery capable of exhibiting all four different performances including enhanced cell internalization, endosomal escape, nuclear localization and targeted delivery into cancerous cells.

Dox and Curcumin (Cur, Figure 1, A and B ) were selected in this study as models of hydrophilic and hydrophobic anticancer drugs, respectively. 20
,
21 Dox-HCl (Adriamycin) is one of the most common chemotherapeutic drugs, classified as a type of anthracycline antibiotic, which result in inhibiting tumor growth by blocking topoisomerase II activity. 22 The natural polyphenol Cur (diferuloylmethane) is a well-known effective anticancer drug due to its anti-oxidant and anti-inflammatory effects which may be used alone or combined with other anticancer clinical drugs. Despite benefits of Cur as an anticancer agent, some limitations such as poor solubility in the aqueous medium and low bioavailability have limited the clinical application of this drug. 23 Therefore, there is a tremendous necessity to utilize a suitable nanocarrier with the affinity of hydrophobic binding to Cur in order to overcome the mentioned drawbacks and improve its therapeutic efficiency and tissue targeting ability.

Non-toxic photoluminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are attractive carbon-based quantum dots which have been considered as new emerging probes for non-invasive tracking in biological systems. GQDs could allow efficient biological tracking due to their high photoluminescent and biocompatibility, chemical and optical stability as well as non-cytotoxicity. 24–26 In addition to cellular tracking, the decoration of peptide-drug complexes by GQDs may also enhance the binding of drugs to peptide by introducing different functional groups.

Thus, in the present study bright blue emitting GQDs have been synthesized by hydrothermal method and subsequently attached to MiRGD peptide-Dox/Cur complexes. The prepared fluorescence nano-composites were qualitatively and quantitatively assessed and their efficiencies for drug delivery into the human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) as a model of αv integrin-over-expressing cancer cells and human foreskin fibroblasts (HFF) as a negative control line were investigated. Finally, the in-vivo biodistribution pattern of Dox/Cur labeled with MiRGD and GQDs was evaluated by administration of free and complexed drug solutions intravenously, into 4T1 tumor-bearing mice.

ce:section.ce:para: E. coli C41 (DE3) pLysS was obtained from Novagen. pET28a expression vector was purchased from Invitrogen. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium F12 (DMEM F12) were purchased from Gibco. Ni-NTA-Sepharose resin was obtained from Qiagen. IPTG was obtained from Vivantis. Dialysis bag (cut-off: 3.5 kDa) was purchased from Sigma. Urea, citric acid and ethanol were purchased from Merck. Sulfuric acid (98%) was obtained from Merck.

Graphene quantum dots were synthesized by dissolving 0.21 g citric acid and 0.18 g urea into 5 ml water, and the mixture was stirred until obtaining a clear solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a teflon, which is inserted in stainless autoclave. After this, the sealed autoclave was heated at 160 °C in an oven for 4 h. Finally, ethanol was added into the solution and the final product was collected by centrifuging at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The solution was dried by rotary evaporator, and subsequently redispersed into deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression and purification of MiRGD peptide
ce:section.ce:para: In order to express MiRGD peptide, the expression vector (pET28a) was transformed into Escherichia coli bl21 (DE3) pLysS. Then, the E. coli was cultured in LB medium containing kanamycin (100 μg/ml) at 37 °C overnight. The amount of 1 ml of pre-cultured bacteria was inoculated in 250 ml of 2xyt medium and incubated at 37 °C under shaking at 200 rpm until the optical density (OD600) reached ~0.9. Then, peptide expression was induced by IPTG to a final concentration of 0.5 mM at 37 °C for 4 h. The bacterial cells were harvested by centrifugation at 5000 RPM for 15 min. The cell pellets were resuspended in lysis buffer [20 mM Tris–HCl, 500 mM NaCl, 8 M urea, and 5 mM imidazole;(pH 11)] and sonicated on ice in 10 cycles of 10 s bursts and 20 s rest intervals. The lysate was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was loaded on a Ni-NTA column chromatography and incubated for 1 h at room temperature. The impurities were removed by six washing buffers with increasing imidazole and decreasing urea concentration [20 mM Tris–HCl, 1.0 M NaCl, Urea (6.0 M, 4.0 M, 2.0 M and 0 M), and imidazole (10 mM and 20 mM); (pH 8)]. The chimeric peptide was eluted by elution buffer [20 mM Tris–HCl, 500 mM NaCl and 250 mM imidazole; (pH 8)]. After observing the peptide bands on 15% sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE), purified peptide was desalted by dialysis (cut-off: 3.5 kDa) against PBS buffer, pH 7.4 at 4 °C for 24 h. Dialysis buffer was exchanged every 6 h. Finally, peptide stocked and stored at −20 °C for subsequent steps.

Stock solution (1 mg/ml) of curcumin was prepared in DMSO and then diluted up to 54.2 μM in PBS. Dox was prepared at a concentration of 36 μM in PBS. The prepared solution of raw drugs was mixed separately with different concentrations of the purified peptide (10.3, 41.5, 72.7 μM). Then, reaction mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. Finally, these mixtures were incubated at room temperature for 2 h.

The concentration of 100 μg/ml GQDs was added to the different concentrations of MiRGD-Cur and MiRGD-Dox complexes according to Tables 2s and 3s, separately. These mixtures were incubated under agitation at 4 °C for 24 h. In order to remove free (un-complexed) particles, the solution was dialyzed by PBS buffer for 24 h in darkness (buffer was replaced by fresh PBS every 6 h).

The drug encapsulation efficiency was investigated by dissolving 100 μl nanocomplexes in 900 μl DMSO, and the solution was shaken for 24 h at RT. The absorbance of the solutions was measured by UV–vis spectroscopy at 480 nm and 430 nm wavelengths, for Dox and Cur, respectively. The amount of drug entrapped was calculated according to the standard curves, then the encapsulation efficiency of drugs in MiRGD was obtained as follows:
Encapsulation efficiency (%) = (entrapped drug/initial drug) × 100.

Drug release at pH 5.5 and 7.4 was accomplished as follows: The amount of 500 μl of GQDs-MiRGD-Cur complex 1 and GQDs-MiRGD-Dox complex 1′ was used for release experiments, respectively. By using dialysis bag (cut-off: 3.5 kDa), the mixture solution was dialyzed in 4 ml of PBS buffer solution with pH of 5.5 and 7.4 under shaking with the rotation speed of 100 rpm at 37 °C (0.2% w/w tween 80% was added to release media of Cur). 1 mL of the release media in the reservoirs was taken out for analysis at time-specified intervals (0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 24, 48, and 72 h) and replaced with an equal volume of fresh media. The absorbance of collected samples was read at 480 nm and 430 nm wavelengths, respectively. Then the release profiles were drawn by plotting the percentage of drug released vs time.

HUVEC and HFF cells were cultured in DMEM F12 medium supplemented with FBS, streptomycin (100 mg/ml) and penicillin (100 U/ml) in 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere at 37 °C. Trypsin (0.25%) was used to digest the cells for 2 min, after 2 days and enable passage.

Fluorescent microscopy was employed to investigate the cellular uptake of free and complexed drugs (Dox/Cur). For this reason, HUVEC and HFF cells were seeded in 24-well plates (1 × 105 cells/well) and incubated at 37 °C. After 24 h, the cells were treated with 40 μl of specified concentrations of Dox/Cur-MiRGD-GQDs nano-composites and free drugs according to Table 3s. After another 24 h, the cells were washed two times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and finally, fluorescence imaging was done by Cytation™ 3 multi-mode detection system. The imaging was performed in the blue spectral region for GQDs and Cur, and green spectral region for Dox visualizing.

The quantitative uptake efficiency of complexed drugs into the desired cells was estimated by fluorimetric assay as a fast, non-expensive and fairly sensitive assay. In this regard, HUVEC and HFF cells in their exponential growth phase were seeded at the density of 1 × 104 per each well of black 96 well cell culture plate (SPL Life Sciences). According to Table 3s, treatment was done in different concentrations of free and complexed Cur/Dox and different volumes (20, 50, 100 μl and 10, 30, 50 μl) of prepared Cur-MiRGD-GQDs and Dox-MiRGD-GQDs nano-composites, respectively. Before visualization with fluorescence microscope, each well was washed slowly two times with PBS. The mode of scanning area defined on BioTek's Gen5™ software of Cytation™ 3 multi-mode detection system was selected for investigation of free and complexed drugs entry into the cells.

4T1-induced breast cancer BALB/c mice (six to eight-weeks-old) were obtained from University of Tehran maintained under the constant condition of temperature (22 °C-24 °C) with a dark/light cycle of 12 h/12 h. All animal experiments were performed in accordance with relevant guidelines and regulations of ethics committee of Tarbiat Modares University. In order to evaluate the bio distribution of free and complexed Dox and Cur, the tumor-bearing mice were divided into four groups as follows: treated with 100 μg/ml of free Dox (40 μg /mouse), complexed Dox (40 μg /mouse Dox equivalent, 5 μM MiRGD and 100 μg/ml GQDs), free Cur (15 μg /mouse) and complexed Cur (15 μg /mouse Cur equivalent, 5 μM MiRGD and 100 μg/ml GQDs) by a single injection via the tail vein. The fluorescence intensities of the complexes (MiRGD-Dox/Cur-GQDs) were detected by using UVITEC Cambridge in-vivo imaging system (model Q9 Alliance) before and after administration. The fluorescence excitation wavelength range was between 460 and 490 nm by choosing epi blue filter and the emission wavelength was 520 nm. The fluorescence imaging was performed in various time after the injection of drug solutions at different exposure times. Finally, the animals were sacrificed and major organs were rapidly removed for ex-vivo imaging while they were fixed in formalin for further analyses.

UV/Vis absorption spectroscopy of synthesized GQDs revealed two peaks at 234 nm and 337 nm. The absorption at 234 nm is related to the π → π ∗ transition which is associated with the C=C bond, and the absorption at 337 nm is due to the n → π ∗ transition of the C=O bond (Figure 1, C).

The bands related to surface functional groups present on GQDs were detected by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Figure 1, D). The broad absorption bands at 3000-3500 cm−1 indicate amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDs. The bands at 1600 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrational absorption band of C=O in COOH and CONH and the band at 1400 cm−1 is related to the bending vibrations of C=C.

The fluorescence spectrum of the synthesized GQDs was investigated at different excitation wavelengths. The obtained results exhibited excitation-independent fluorescence of GQDs. So that, by changing the excitation wavelength from 340 nm to 410 nm, fluorescence intensity was changed, as a maximum emission was observed at 450 nm when excited at wavelength 360 nm (Figure 1, E).

As previously described, the coding sequence of MiRGD peptide in pET28a expression vector was used for expression in E. coli BL21 (DE3) pLysS. His-tag tail at both N- and C-terminal of peptide allow purification by Ni-NTA-Sepharose chromatography under urea gradient. 11

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the formation of MiRGD-Dox/MiRGD-Cur complexes

ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence spectra of Cur and Dox as naturally fluorescent drugs were assessed in the presence of various concentrations of MiRGD to verify the efficient interactions between the drugs and peptide in the prepared Cur/Dox-MiRGD complexes. The emission spectrums were recorded with excitation at 420 nm and 480 nm which are the maximum excitation wavelengths of Cur and Dox, respectively. As shown in Figure 2, A and B in the presence of different concentrations of MiRGD (10.3, 41.5 and 72.7 μM), significant changes occur in the fluorescence spectra of both complexed Cur and Dox, respectively. As Figure 2, A exhibits a broad and comparatively weak fluorescence band was observed for free Cur in aqueous media, while it was increased significantly and shifted to shorter wavelengths (blue shift) by increasing the MiRGD concentration from 10.3 to 72.7 μM. The fluorescence spectroscopy clearly indicates the Cur microenvironment changes upon conjugation with MiRGD peptide. The fluorescence of curcumin showed a polarity solvent-dependent shift in the emission maximum. A blue shift in the emission maximum along with an increased fluorescence intensity indicated displacement of curcumin from a polar to a less polar environment and indicated that the binding of curcumin was hydrophobic in nature. Previous studies carried out on the interaction of Cur with proteins have demonstrated an increase and blue shift in fluorescence signal due to Cur binding to hydrophobic regions of protein molecules. 27
Figure 2, B shows the increase of the Dox emission intensity by increasing the MiRGD concentration in the prepared complexes without any shift, similar to the previous studies. 28

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulation efficiency of drugs in MiRGD
ce:section.ce:para: The encapsulation efficiency in MiRGD was obtained 70% and 43% for Dox and Cur, respectively.

The fluorescence properties of GQDs were investigated to explore the interactions between GQDs and Dox-MiRGD /Cur-MiRGD complexes. As shown in Figures 2, C, D and S1, A, B, the intensity of GQDs fluorescence decreased after interaction with Dox/Cur-MiRGD complexes and by increasing the MiRGD concentration in complex from 10.3 to 72.7 μM (Tables S1 and S2), the fluorescence intensity was further decreased. This phenomenon might have occurred due to the electron transfer through fluorescence quenching as a consequence of drugs attachment onto the GQDs surface. Moreover, the fluorescence spectrum of complexed Cur with MiRGD and GQDs showed a significant blue-shift compared to free Cur at ex: 430 nm and em: 540 nm (Figure S2). These results confirmed that GQDs noncovalently interact with Cur/Dox- MiRGD complexes. In addition, the encapsulation efficiency of Cur and Dox into the triple complexes of Dox-MiRGD-GQDs/Cur-MiRGD-GQDs was increased compared to Dox-MiRGD/Cur-MiRGD complexes. Enhanced π-π stacking and other noncovalent interactions could increase the calculated encapsulation efficiency of the drugs into the triple complexes consisting of GQDs to 70% for Cur and 78% for Dox (~27% and ~8% more than corresponding double complexes, respectively).

The ζ-potential is an important physicochemical parameter for nano drug delivery systems. Electrostatic interactions between nanoparticles (drugs and nano-carriers) by variation in the surface charges cause change in ζ-potential values. The ζ-potential of MiRGD and GQDs was +12.2 mV and −17 mV, respectively (Figure 2, E). However, triple complexes of MiRGD-Dox-GQDs and MiRGD-Cur-GQDs demonstrated the different ζ-potential values of +6 mV and −1.72 mV, respectively. The occurred changes in the ζ-potential of the prepared complexes reveal that electrostatic interactions have been established.

TEM microscopy was performed to further investigate the morphology and size distribution of GQDs and prepared nanocomposites. The average diameter of GQDs was about 10 nm (Figure 2, F) and the average size of triple nano-composites of Dox-peptide-GQDs and Cur-peptide-GQDs was about 50 and 100 nm, respectively (Figure 2, F II and III). The observation of bright cauliflower-like structures on dark dots can indicate the attachment of Cur-peptide and Dox-peptide complexes to GQDs and confirm the formation of triple complexes of drug, peptide and GQDs.

The results of 96 h release profile of Cur and Dox from the prepared nano-composites at two different pH (7.4 and 5.5) are presented in Figure 3 . The initial burst release of Dox was observed and then the total amount of released Dox was increased over time with a gentle slope and reached 47% and 77% at pH 7.4 and 5.5, respectively, after 72 h. However, Cur was released with a slower rate than Dox, up to 21% and 73% at pH 7.4 and 5, respectively. Interestingly, the results clearly showed that the pH value of the medium had a remarkable effect on the drug release rate from nanocomplexes and the release of both drugs was higher in the acidic condition (pH 5.5). The controlled release was observed for both pH-sensitive drug systems; however, none of them did not reach to 100% release which might be due to the remaining electrostatic interactions and π-π stacking between different parts of Dox/Cur structures and other components of the nano-composites after the investigated time periods.

The quantitative analysis of cellular uptake of prepared complexes was carried out by fluorimetric assay at three different excitation and emission wavelengths for GQDs (ex: 360 nm, em: 390 nm), Dox (ex: 480 nm, em: 600 nm) and Cur (ex: 430 nm, em: 540 nm). The fluorimetric data confirmed the previous results obtained from cellular uptake assay, as the HUVEC cells treated with 20 μl of Cur-nanocomposite 3 (Table 3s) at both wavelengths of ex: 360 nm, em: 390 nm and ex: 430 nm, em: 540 nm related to maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs and Cur, respectively, and 50 μl of Dox- nanocomposite 2 and 10 μl of Dox- nanocomposite 3 (Table. S3) at wavelengths of ex: 480 nm, em: 600 nm and ex: 360 nm, em: 390 nm related to maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of Dox and GQDs, respectively, showed the highest fluorescence intensity. Results showed that the mean fluorescence intensity of Cur and Dox in HUVEC cells treated with Cur-nanocomposite 3/Dox-nanocomposite 2 and 3 increased about two-fold compared to free Cur and Dox, respectively. Interestingly the results showed that treated HFF cells with the same amounts of the complexes revealed appreciably lower fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity of Cur-nanocomposite 3 in treated HUVEC cells increased about four-fold and Dox-nanocomposite 2 and 3 elevated about two-fold compared to HFF cells, respectively. In addition, treated cells with either free Cur/Dox, GQDs or MiRGD peptide didn't show significant differences with untreated cells. Typical data and selected wells as well as dot plots of the average fluorescence signal of the triplicate wells are shown in Figures 6, A, B and S3, S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vivo bio distribution and tumor targeting
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the biodistribution pattern of Dox/Cur labeled with MiRGD and GQDs in-vivo, the free and complexed drug solutions were intravenously administrated into 4T1 tumor-bearing mice. As shown in Figure 7, A the fluorescence results showed that in comparison to the free drugs, the accumulation of complexes was significantly increased in the tumor site followed up to 2.5 h after injection. The accumulation of Dox complex in tumor was substantially higher than Cur complex (Figure 7, B) probably, due to the rapid bladder clearance of Cur at the evaluated concentration. The fluorescence imaging of Dox peptideticle in tumor-bearing mouse in different light exposure times within 2.5 h post injection is shown in Figure S5.

To further investigate the distribution of complexes and free drugs in various organs, the animals were sacrificed immediately 2.5 h after intravenous injection. As shown in Figure 7, B, the Dox/Cur complexes, but not free drugs, were significantly accumulated in tumor sites. The accumulation of complexed Dox was dramatically higher than complexed Cur. In addition, the trace amounts of the drugs were detected in liver, lung, spleen, kidneys and heart. Hence, MiRGD peptide and GQDs-modified chemotherapeutic drugs of Dox and Cur endow them with tumor-targeting specificity and enhance the visualizing complexes in tumor site.

The major disadvantage of conventional chemotherapy is lack of specificity to target tumor cells. 30
,
31 Nowadays, bio-engineered technologies are in progress to introduce new drug delivery systems for controlled targeted delivery of chemotherapeutic agents without having any undesirable and severe side effects on normal cells. 32 The current tendency of bio-researchers is focused on chimeric peptides as efficient nanocarriers to deliver a variety of biologically active molecules into the cells. Therefore, in this study, a previously designed chimeric peptide (MiRGD) 11 was used as a multifunctional amphipathic carrier for the delivery of both hydrophilic (Dox) and hydrophobic (Cur) drugs in-vitro and in-vivo conditions.

Overall, peptides are suitable group of biocompatible nano-carriers for therapeutic agents and imaging probes and their small size allow them to penetrate deep tissues. 33 MiRGD peptide exhibits amphipathic nature and binds Dox and Cur to its hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions, respectively. Therefore, MiRGD peptide overcomes Cur's solubility limitation in aqueous media. Several studies were carried out on preparation of Cur/Dox in complex with peptides. For instance, Cur encapsulation in MAX8 peptide hydrogel 27 brought about an increase in fluorescence intensity and blue shift because of Cur binding to hydrophobic regions of the peptide, similar to our observation (Figure S2, A). However, in another study, Cur was trapped in self-assembled dipeptide nanoparticles, and the emission intensity of Cur in the complex was decreased compared to free Cur. 34 It seems that depending on the availability of trapped Cur as a natural fluorescent agent in the complex, its fluorescence intensity can increase or decrease. In another study, conjugation of Dox with RU006 peptide increased Dox emission intensity in the complex compared to Dox alone. 28 Similarly, in a study conducted by Karavasili et al loading of Dox and Cur on a peptide hydrogel caused a decrease in the fluorescence intensity. 35 Therefore, it can be concluded that MiRGD multifunctional peptide upon simple mixing with both hydrophobic and hydrophilic drugs showed changes in fluorescence intensity which can be used as a simple indicator for dual complex formation as reported earlier.

In this study, in order to make a theranostic peptideticle, the prepared dual complexes were decorated with GQDs as a non-toxic high-sensitive fluorescence nanoprobe, which allows tracking of chemotherapeutic agents. Moreover, GQDs possess large surface area to volume and by introducing different functional groups and binding sites on a planar structure allow several types of non-covalent interactions such as electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding with different functional groups of peptide and Dox/ Cur as well as π-π stacking between aromatic ring structures. 25
,
36 Hence, in addition to cellular tracking, the decoration of peptide-drug complexes by GQDs may also enhance the binding of drugs to peptide. Several studies have shown that the drug release occurs from GQDs-based platforms by EPR effect, specific ligands or diffusion and subsequently up taken by cells. 37

The results of release assay of Cur and Dox from the prepared ternary nano-composites (Figure 3) at two different pH (7.4 and 5.5) demonstrated that pH had a remarkable effect on the drug release rate from nano-composites and the release of both drugs was higher in the acidic condition (pH 5.5). Moreover, Cur due to its hydrophobic nature and its low solubility in the aqueous medium was released with a slower rate than Dox. Similar to our results, a graphene oxide (GO)/magnetic nanoparticle (MNP) @ polyamidoamine (PAMAM) platform as a nanocarrier for loading Dox and Cur reported that Dox and Cur have been released from GO/MNP@PAMAM platform up to 37%, 18.7%, at pH 7.4 after 72 h, respectively, and the drugs release reached up to 80% and 33.8% for Dox and Cur, at pH 5, respectively. 38 Interestingly, the release of hydrophobic Cur from our prepared nano-composites reached 73% at pH 5, after 72 h (Figure 3), which was significantly more than its release from their presented platform (33.8%). In another study, Cur release from Trp-GQDs at pH 7.4 and 5.5 at 37 °C has been investigated and the results shown that the pH variation had no remarkable effect on the Cur release during the first hours. However, the amounts of Cur release reached 34% and 19% at pH 5.5 and 7.4 after 150 h, respectively. Therefore, similar to our data, their results demonstrated that the amount of Cur release was higher in acidic media compared to neutral media. 39

Moreover, as we previously demonstrated that GQDs efficiently enhance the internalization of DNA plasmid into HEK 293T cells, 10 they also could increase the internalization of loaded drugs into the cells by hydrophobic interactions with cellular membrane. In a study, Cur was attached to GQDs and demonstrated higher anticancer activity. 40 In the present study, the ternary complexes were formed by introducing GQDs to the solutions of prepared dual complexes of Dox/Cur-MiRGD under mild agitation at 4 °C, overnight, to ensure enough time for efficient slow interactions between GQDs with different functional groups of dual complexes. We showed that the intensity of GQDs emission spectrum significantly decreased after interaction with Dox/Cur-MiRGD complexes, and by increasing the peptide concentration from 10.3 to 72.7 μM the fluorescence intensity was further decreased. A previous study has shown a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs in a prepared platform of Cur attached with GQDs by stirring for 30 min at 4 °C, owing to the strong π–π interaction between phenolic groups of Cur and aromatic structures in graphene derivatives. 41 Similar to our results, Dong et al demonestrated that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the complex of Dox-GQDs-RGD was decreased. 26

The results obtained from in-vitro study demonstrated that conjugation with MiRGD and GQDs increased the cellular uptake of both Cur and Dox (Figure 4). Recently, several studies carried out on Dox and Cur conjugated with different CPPs in order to enhance drug delivery. For instance, similar to our results, it has been shown that cellular uptake of cyclic peptide [W(RW)4]-Dox was higher than free Dox, in SKOV-3 cells after 24 h. 42 Likewise, the conjugation of Dox with TAT, poly-L-arginine, KRP and penetration has shown the same results in several cancer models. 43 Walker et al demonstrated that conjugation of Dox to CPPs of SynB1 and ELP two-fold increased the tumor inhibition than Dox alone, under hyperthermia condition. 44 In another study, it has been shown that TAT-conjugated mesoporous silica nanoparticles enhance the intracellular and intranuclear Dox delivery in breast cancer cells more than free Dox. 45 Nuclear localizing peptide conjugated with redox-sensitive polymersomes enhanced delivery of Cur and Dox to pancreatic cancer cells and significantly reduced the viability of cells. 46 Moreover, we have shown the intracellular uptake of GNP conjugated to MCaUF1-9(Ala), a CPP derived from maurocalcine (MCa) animal toxin, and compared it with TAT functionalized GNP. 47

MiRGD as a CPP peptide containing NLS enhances the delivery of chemotherapeutic drugs into nucleus. In addition to NLS, MiRGD peptide contains the iRGD sequence with binding affinity to αv integrin, significantly enhanced the uptake of both investigated drugs to αv integrin over-expressed HUVEC cells. Results showed that the mean fluorescence intensity of Cur and Dox uptaken by HUVEC cells treated with Cur-nanocomposite 3/Dox-nanocomposite 2 and 3 increased about two-fold compared to free Cur and Dox, respectively. So far, RGD has been combined with several different nanoparticles including proteins, peptides, liposomes, micelles and etc.
48 It has been reported that RGD-modified lipid-polymer nanoparticles increased the integrin receptor-positive cellular uptake, which is similar to our in-vitro results (Figures 4 and 5). Moreover, they also claimed that their RGD-modified nanoparticles could improve the poor water solubility of Cur. 49 Besides all the unique features, MiRGD contains the fusogenic peptide that is capable to facilitate the endosomal scape of the conjugated drugs and thereupon enhance the functionality of drug delivery of the nano-carrier. Yamada et al introduced a transferrin-modified liposomes attached to GALA fusogenic peptide to delivery of mastoparan into the cytosol of K562 cells. 50

Although there are numerous in-vitro assessments on a wide variety of biomaterials conjugated with RGD peptide, in-vivo studies generated more variable results. 51 Interestingly, in this study, the results obtained from biodistribution and in-vivo study confirmed the in-vitro assessment and revealed that Dox/Cur delivery by MiRGD and GQDs leads to an improved tumor accumulation (Figures 7 and S5). These observations corroborated that the iRGD genetically inserted -MiRGD leads to an enhanced tumor penetration through αvβ3 integrin binding which has been found to be overexpressed on the surface of 4T1 mice breast cancer cell line injected in BALB/c mice subcutaneously.

To the best of our knowledge, the present study is the first one conducted using multi-functional chimeric peptides as a carrier for drug delivery capable of exhibiting four different performances together including enhanced cell internalization, endosomal escape, nuclear localization and targeted delivery into cancerous cells. Altogether, the in-vitro and in-vivo results obtained from the present study introduce a nanocarrier based on MiRGD and GQDs, for chemotherapeutic drugs delivery into cancerous cells in order to overcome the drawbacks of conventional chemotherapy. According to the results presented in this article, both Cur and Dox as a hydrophobic and hydrophilic drug, respectively, can be efficiently loaded in a developed potential drug delivery system based on MiRGD peptide and GQDs. Multifunctional MiRGD peptide allows drug packaging, cell internalization, endosomal escape, as well as targeted delivery of the therapeutic drugs to αv integrin-over-expressed cells. GQDs could enhance cell internalization and efficient tracking of cargo. Although the peptideticles prepared in this study could be novel suitable drug delivery systems in both in vitro and in vivo conditions, further investigations are required to their employment for chemotherapeutic drugs delivery and also co-delivery of multiple drugs for combinational chemotherapy of cancer.
Soroush Moasses Ghafary; Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization.
Elnaz Rahimjazi; Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation.
Hadiseh Hamzehil; Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation.
Sayed Mostafa Modarres Mousavi; Methodology, Investigation.
Maryam Nikkhah; Methodology, Validation, Supervision.
Saman Hosseinkhani; Project administration, Validation, Granting, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support of this work was provided by a grant (#IG/39803) from research council of Tarbiat Modares University to the cell death and differentiation research program and a grant from Council for Development of Stem Cell Sciences and Technologies.
